critique.summary: The article reports a bug in macOS 26.3.1 where the /etc/resolver/ mechanism for custom DNS suffixes no longer works for non-IANA root zone TLDs. The system's mDNSResponder intercepts queries for these custom TLDs and treats them as multicast DNS, bypassing the specified unicast DNS servers and causing resolution failures for local development and private network domains.
mainPoints: macOS 26.3.1 breaks the /etc/resolver/ supplemental DNS feature for custom TLDs not in the IANA root zone.
mDNSResponder intercepts queries for these custom/private TLDs and handles them as mDNS, ignoring unicast DNS servers specified in /etc/resolver/ files.
This change breaks local development workflows relying on dnsmasq or similar local DNS servers for domains like .internal, .test, .home.arpa, .lan, and arbitrary custom TLDs.
Standard TLDs like google.com and bbc.co.uk continue to resolve correctly.
The only workaround is manually adding entries to /etc/hosts, which is impractical for dynamic environments.
The issue was confirmed by tests showing no DNS queries reaching local dnsmasq and mDNSResponder returning cached negative responses with large TTLs.
The problem represents a regression from macOS 25.x where these setups worked correctly.
critique.truthfulness: The claims in the article are consistent with documented macOS behavior and developer reports. The /etc/resolver/ mechanism is a known macOS feature for per-domain DNS resolution, and the described regression aligns with changes in mDNSResponder's handling of special-use and custom TLDs. The article provides detailed reproduction steps, diagnostic commands, and references to official documentation and RFCs, supporting the accuracy of the reported bug. However, the article is a user-generated bug report on GitHub Gist and does not cite multiple independent sources or official Apple statements, so while the technical details appear credible and verifiable, the broader impact and official recognition of the bug are not confirmed.
considerations: Apple's official stance or response to this DNS regression is not included and would be important for understanding if this is intentional behavior or a bug to be fixed.
Potential security or privacy reasons behind mDNSResponder's change in handling custom TLDs are not discussed.
The impact on enterprise or non-developer users relying on custom DNS configurations is not explored.
Alternative DNS resolution mechanisms or future macOS updates that might address or mitigate this issue are not mentioned.
The article does not clarify the version numbering confusion between macOS and Darwin kernel versions, which may affect user understanding.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on issues introduced by macOS 26, particularly breaking custom DNS settings and developer workflows. Several users share detailed examples of problems, workarounds, and frustrations with Apple's changes, while others discuss alternative approaches and broader opinions on macOS.

comments.author: alin23
comments.text:
macOS 26 has to be the most breaking version so far, its problems and intended breaking changes making my app dev life so hard this year. Just to name a few:

- Reference Presets no longer allow setting arbitrary SDR nits, making it impossible to natively unlock 1600nits of brightness on MacBook Pros or 2000nits on Studio Display XDR which breaks my Lunar app [0] (this seems to be intended, no idea what hurt Apple that they had to block this under SIP)

- The orange microphone dot indicator and its very colored friends can no longer have their brightness changed for dimming them, which made my YellowDot app useless [1] (I guess this is for privacy, I still think this could have a setting guarded under TouchID like Accessibility Permissions works)

- Floating non-titled windows don't accept mouse events (thankfully this got fixed) [2]

- Gamma table changes don't work on MacBook Neo and M5 Pro/Max which breaks Sub-zero Dimming and dimming external monitors that don't support DDC (thankfully, Apple is looking into it) [3]

- The resizing area thing on very rounded windows which drives everyone nuts, I had to add custom resize handlers to some of my windows

- The `com.apple.SwiftUI.Drag-` temporary file paths that get generated for any file that gets dragged from a drag&drop handler which makes it impossible to get to the original file when dragging images from Clop [4] or file shelf apps like Yoink, Dropover etc.

- NSImage returning different pixel count for .size than what the image actually has, breaking workflows that depended on that to determine the image DPI

[0] https://lunar.fyi/#xdr

[1] https://github.com/FuzzyIdeas/YellowDot/issues/18

[2] https://developer.apple.com/forums//thread/814798

[3] https://developer.apple.com/forums/thread/819331

[4] https://lowtechguys.com/clop
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed, specific list of issues with macOS 26, supported by links and examples, indicating a well-informed and accurate perspective on the update's impact.

comments.author: patabyte
comments.text:
Interesting - I run a nearly identical set, with many TLDs configured in `/etc/resolver/X` and dnsmasq handling the resolve and I have not had a single issue.

the resolver confs all contain this content:

    # /etc/resolver/example-private
    nameserver: 127.0.0.1
    domain example-private
I noticed in the author's bug report they do not include `domain`, which is documented in `man 5 resolver` as:

    # The domain directive is only necessary, if your local
    # router advertises something like localdomain and you have
    # set up your hostnames via an external domain.
In the real world though, I've found the `domain` setting to be required nearly every time. I wonder if adding it will resolve the issue?
comments.analysis: This comment offers a practical insight into DNS configuration on macOS, suggesting a potential cause for the reported issues and a possible solution, demonstrating technical knowledge.

comments.author: philo23
comments.text:
It's not quite the same, but I've moved to using *.localhost for all my local web dev work. All modern browsers will resolve *.localhost to 127.0.0.1 internally. No need to setup any DNS resolvers or edit your hosts file.

But that only really helps you when you're dealing with websites in a browser, and when you want the address to resolve back to your local machine. So it wont help you with other programs like python/wget/etc or any calls you make to getaddrinfo()
comments.analysis: This comment provides a useful alternative for local development DNS resolution that avoids some of the issues with custom DNS settings, though it notes limitations for non-browser applications.

comments.author: binaryturtle
comments.text:
I run a setup like that on my (outdated) Yosemite machine to provide multiple private TLDs for local deployment/development needs.

I set that up in like 2014? Even back then it was known already that the quick /etc/resolver way was the deprecated way to do things. So I guess they finally killed that feature off?

The proper (more awkward) way is to use scutil directly (which then stores the settings in some binary plist somewhere, I assume).

Maybe try this and see if it still works afterwards?
comments.analysis: This comment adds historical context and suggests that the traditional method of setting custom DNS resolvers in /etc/resolver is deprecated, implying that changes in macOS 26 might be a continuation of this trend.

comments.author: mrbuttons454
comments.text:
Papercuts like this are why I moved away from macOS.

I will say, I don't love the use of LLMs to write these bug reports. It's probably fine if reviewed, but at least review for things like "worked on macOS 25", which obviously didn't exist. If that wasn't caught, how sure are you that the rest of the report is accurate? We all want the bugs fixed, but people are going to start throwing out the obviously LLM written reports rather than have to validate each claim, since the author probably didn't.
comments.analysis: The comment expresses frustration with macOS issues and skepticism about the accuracy of LLM-generated bug reports, raising a valid concern about the reliability of automated reports but with some emotional bias.

comments.author: pfortuny
comments.text: Lo and behold, just FYI. Trying to help.
comments.analysis: A brief comment aimed at correcting a phrase usage; while polite and constructive, it adds little substantive value to the technical discussion.

comments.author: JimmaDaRustla
comments.text: Again? This happened like 6 or 7 years ago. I had so many issues with macOS in the few years I was forced to use a MacBook that I refused to use it. Not surprised to see this stuff still happening.
comments.analysis: This comment reflects personal frustration and historical perspective on recurring macOS issues, which is likely accurate but lacks technical detail or constructive suggestions.

comments.author: bpicolo
comments.text:
Another funny thing about Mac networking.

There's a game I play (Old School Runescape) that does network ticks every .6s. Mac does some sort of aggressive optimization on the network hardware/software, so network this infrequent doesn't keep the layers "hot", and you end up getting delayed ticks regularly, meaning you learn what should be happening in the game .2-.5s late. This optimization for (I assume) battery life makes the software not work as intended.

Playing anything that streams, like video, or triggering TCP connections (e.g. curl) at a more frequent clip while the game is running fixes the problem.

No way other than hacks that I've found to fix it, and I have no idea how you could report this to the right team at Apple to get it actually fixed.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed example of macOS network optimizations causing unintended side effects for certain applications, showing technical insight though it lacks a clear solution.

comments.author: intrasight
comments.text:
Honest question: How would this affect me and the vast majority of macOS users who use the device for media consumption and productivity applications?

Next question: what reason would Apple have to make a change that would interfere with developers using their operating system?
comments.analysis: This comment raises reasonable questions about the impact of the changes on typical users and Apple's motivations, encouraging reflection though it does not provide direct technical insight.
